title: Jamaican cornmeal porridge
subtitle: Thick, spiced cornmeal porridge with condensed milk
excerpt: Fine cornmeal cooked slowly with coconut milk, condensed milk, cinnamon, nutmeg, and vanilla until thick enough to hold its shape in a bowl. The texture is somewhere between polenta and very thick porridge, smooth, heavy, and deeply comforting. Sweeter and richer than oat porridge, and spiced with warming notes that make it a specific kind of breakfast rather than a generic start to the day.
sourceNotes: Cornmeal porridge is a staple breakfast across the Caribbean, with variations in Jamaica, Trinidad, and Barbados. The Jamaican version uses coconut milk and condensed milk as the liquid base, giving a richness distinct from the water-based versions common in other islands. It is served as a school breakfast, a restorative food, and a Sunday morning dish throughout the Caribbean diaspora.

content.text: Cornmeal porridge thickens significantly as it sits. If it becomes very thick before serving, stir in a splash of warm water or milk and reduce the heat. The final consistency should be thick enough that a spoon dragged through the centre leaves a clear trail for a few seconds before filling in.

Stir constantly for the first 5 minutes after adding the cornmeal to prevent lumps forming at the base of the pan. Once it has thickened and is moving slowly, reduce to the lowest heat and stir every minute or two. The porridge will stick and scorch if left unattended over high heat.

Bring 400 ml of water, the {{coconut-milk}}, {{cinnamon-stick}}, and a pinch of {{sea-salt-fine}} to a simmer in a heavy-bottomed pan. Slowly pour in the {{polenta}} in a thin stream, whisking constantly to prevent lumps. Reduce the heat to low. Cook, stirring frequently with a wooden spoon, for 15 minutes until the porridge is thick and pulling away from the sides of the pan. Add the {{condensed-milk}}, {{nutmeg}}, and {{vanilla-extract}}. Stir well and taste, adjust sweetness with a little more condensed milk if needed. Remove the cinnamon stick and serve.

items.fix: Add the cornmeal in a thin, slow stream while whisking. If lumps form, take the pan off the heat and whisk vigorously, lumps at an early stage can usually be smoothed out
items.cause: Cornmeal added too fast or too much at once

items.fix: Stir in warm water or milk a tablespoon at a time until the desired consistency returns. Porridge continues to thicken as it cools
items.cause: Cooked for too long or too little liquid

items.fix: Cornmeal porridge requires low heat and regular stirring. If the base has caught, pour the unscorched portion into a clean pan without scraping the bottom
items.cause: Heat too high and stirring infrequent

content.text: Cornmeal porridge is the Caribbean breakfast at its best: comforting, cheap, and filling. In Jamaica it is the school-morning breakfast, the sick-day comfort, and the Sunday treat made with extra condensed milk. The mix of coconut milk, cinnamon, and condensed milk is specific to Jamaica and the Eastern Caribbean. The Barbadian version uses more water and less coconut milk. In the Caribbean diaspora in Britain it remains a home-cooking staple, made for children as a substantial breakfast before a long day.
